coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive immunoassay of carcinoma antigen 125 in untreated human plasma samples using gold nanoparticles with flower like morphology: A new platform in early stage diagnosis of ovarian cancer and efficient management
coredata.dc:creator: Hasanzadeh, Mohammad
Sahmani, Rahimeh
Solhi, Elham
Mokhtarzadeh, Ahad
Shadjou, Nasrin
Mahboob, Soltanali
coredata.dc:description: Ovarian cancer, as one of the most life-threatening malignancies among women worldwide, is usually diagnosed at the late stage despite up regulation of molecular markers such as carcinoma antigen 125 (CA 125) at the early stages of the malignancy. CA 125 is the only tumor marker recommended for clinical use in the diagnosis and management of ovarian cancer. The potential role of CA-125 for the early detection of ovarian cancer is controversial and has not yet been adopted for widespread screening efforts in asymptomatic women. Therefore, early detection of CA 125 in human biofluids is highly demanded. In the present study, a novel method was proposed for the fabrication of electrochemical immunosensor based reduced graphene oxide (RGO). Cysteamine capped gold nanoparticle (Cys-AuNPs) were deposited over the surface of ERGO probe using electrophoretic deposition method. These Cys-AuNPs/ERGO probes provide the favorable sites to attach the monoclonal antibody specific to CA 125 antigen. Cyclic voltammetry (CV), and square wave voltammetry (SWV) were applied for the electrochemical recognition of the biolayer. The represented signals demonstrates excellent figure of merits and good capability of the engineered immunosensor towards sensitive detection of CA 125. Quantitative measurements of CA 125 in human plasma samples have been demonstrated, showing the potential of the practical application of this novel immunosensor for the analysis of this biomarker in blood serum samples. This immunosensor has the ability of direct electron transfer as compared to earlier reported electrochemical immunosensors based electrochemical methods. Further, this immunosensor provides a very suitable and convenient alternative to replace the expensive commercially available methods such as immunohistochemistry. The following regression equation between the electrochemical current response and the CA 125 concentration range from 0.1 to 400 U/mL was found. The low limit of quantification for this immunosensor was 0.1 U/mL. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first reported on the direct immobilization of antibody on the surface of Cys-AuNPs/ERGO for fabrication of immunosensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ovarian cancer
Immunoassay
Carcinoma antigen 125
Antibody-antigen interaction
Gold nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analytical characteristic of the CA 125 immunosensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support by Tabriz University of Medical Sciences .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) CVs for 0.005 g dopamine containing 5 mL of 0.1 M PBS (pH = 7.4) using GCE (no cycle = 5, sweep rate is 100 mV/s, potential rage is; −0.6 to 0.5 V vs. Ag/AgCl). B) CVs P(DA)-GCE for 20 cycles at a scan rate of 10 mV/s in a potential range from −1.4 to 0 V in a 1 mM GO solution. C) Chronoamperogram for the electrochemical deposition of Au NPs on the surface of Cys A/ERGO/P(DA)/GCE. The applied potential E = 0 V vs. Ag/AgCl and the record time was 300 s.

A) FE-SEM images of P(DA) on the surface of GCE in different magnification. Inset is FE-SEM image of bare GCE. B) FE-SEM images of ERGO on the surface of P(DA)-GCE in two magnification. C) FE-SEM images of Au NPs on the surface of Cys A/ERGO/P(DA)/GCE in two magnification. D) FE-SEM images of CA 125 antibody immobilized on the surface of Au NPs/Cys A/ERGO/P(DA)/GCE in two magnification.

SWVs of GCE, P(DA)-GCE, ERGO-P(DA)-GCE, Cys A/ERGO-P(DA)-GCE, Au NPs/Cys A/ERGO-P(DA)-GCE, Ab/Au NPs/Cys A/ERGO-P(DA)-GCE, and BSA/Ab/Au NPs/Cys A/ERGO-P(DA)-GCE, CA 125/BSA/Ab/Au NPs/Cys A/ERGO-P(DA)-GCE in the solution containing 0.1 M (RF and NaCl). Step potential = 0.001, amplitude time = 0.02 s, frequency = 10 Hz, scan rate = 0.05 V/s.
A–C) CVs of GCE electrode modified by P(DA), P(DA)-ERGO, and Au NPs-Cys A/P(DA)-ERGO-Cys A-Au NPs in 0.01 mM riboflavin, respectively. D) Dependency of peak current versus sweep rates.

A) SWVs of BSA/Ab/Au NPs/Cys A/ERGO-P(DA)-GCE in different concentration of Ca 125 (A: 0, 0.1, 0.34, 0.7, 1.25, and 7.5 U/mL and B: 15, 50, 100, 200, and 400 U/mL) in the solution containing 1 mM RF and 1 mM NaCl. Step potential = 0.001, amplitude time = 0.02 s, frequency = 10 Hz, scan rate = 0.05 V/s. C and D) Calibration curves in different concentrations profile.

A & B) SWVs of BSA/Ab/Au NPs/Cys A/ERGO-P(DA)-GCE in different concentrations of CA 125 in human plasma samples (A) 15, 50, 100, and 250 U/mL and B) 0.1, 0.34, 0.75, 1.25, and 7.5 U/mL). Supporting electrolyte is 0.01 M [RF, KCl]. SWV parameters: potential range of −0.9 V to 0.3 V with a step potential of 5 mV, amplitude 20 mV and frequency 25 Hz. (C & D) Calibration curves of the BSA/Ab/Au NPs/Cys A/ERGO-P(DA)-GCE in human plasma samples.
A & B) CVs and histogram of Au NPs-Cys A/ERGO/P(DA)-GCE in different cycle No. C & D) SWVs and histogram of Au NPs-Cys A/ERGO/P(DA)-GCE in different cycle no.

A) SWVs CA 125/BSA/Ab/Au NPs/Cys A/ERGO-P(DA)-GCE in the presence of different interferent species (BSA, p53, CA 15-3, PSA proteins) in the 0.01 M (RF, NaCl). SWV parameters: potential range of −0.9 V to 0.3 V with a step potential of 5 mV, step potential = 0.001, amplitude time = 0.02 s, frequency = 10 Hz, scan rate = 0.05 V/s. Supporting CA 125 concentration is 100 U/mL.
Possible mechanism for electropolymerization of P(DA).
A comparison of the performance of several immunosensor for the detection of CA 125.

row.entry: Surface plasmon resonance and capacitive immunosensors label-free

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery analysis of the CA-125 specific immunosensor in healthy human samples.

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive immunoassay of carcinoma antigen 125 in untreated human plasma samples using gold nanoparticles with flower like morphology: A new platform in early stage diagnosis of ovarian cancer and efficient management

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry, Higher Education Institute of Rab-Rashid, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Immunology Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanochemistry, Nano Technology Research Center, Uremia University, Uremia 57154, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz 51664, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ovarian cancer, as one of the most life-threatening malignancies among women worldwide, is usually diagnosed at the late stage despite up regulation of molecular markers such as carcinoma antigen 125 (CA 125) at the early stages of the malignancy. CA 125 is the only tumor marker recommended for clinical use in the diagnosis and management of ovarian cancer. The potential role of CA-125 for the early detection of ovarian cancer is controversial and has not yet been adopted for widespread screening efforts in asymptomatic women. Therefore, early detection of CA 125 in human biofluids is highly demanded. In the present study, a novel method was proposed for the fabrication of electrochemical immunosensor based reduced graphene oxide (RGO). Cysteamine capped gold nanoparticle (Cys-AuNPs) were deposited over the surface of ERGO probe using electrophoretic deposition method. These Cys-AuNPs/ERGO probes provide the favorable sites to attach the monoclonal antibody specific to CA 125 antigen. Cyclic voltammetry (CV), and square wave voltammetry (SWV) were applied for the electrochemical recognition of the biolayer. The represented signals demonstrates excellent figure of merits and good capability of the engineered immunosensor towards sensitive detection of CA 125. Quantitative measurements of CA 125 in human plasma samples have been demonstrated, showing the potential of the practical application of this novel immunosensor for the analysis of this biomarker in blood serum samples. This immunosensor has the ability of direct electron transfer as compared to earlier reported electrochemical immunosensors based electrochemical methods. Further, this immunosensor provides a very suitable and convenient alternative to replace the expensive commercially available methods such as immunohistochemistry. The following regression equation between the electrochemical current response and the CA 125 concentration range from 0.1 to 400 U/mL was found. The low limit of quantification for this immunosensor was 0.1 U/mL. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first reported on the direct immobilization of antibody on the surface of Cys-AuNPs/ERGO for fabrication of immunosensors.

ce:section.ce:para: Carcinoma antigen 125 (CA 125) is a protein that in humans is encoded by the MUC16 gene [1,2]. CA-125 has found application as a tumor marker or biomarker that may be elevated in the blood of some patients with specific types of cancers [3–7], or other conditions that are benign. CA 125 is the only tumor marker recommended for clinical use in the diagnosis and management of ovarian cancer. CA-125 is the most frequently used biomarker for ovarian cancer detection [8]. Medical societies including American Congress of Obstetricians and Gynecologists recommend against women with average risk of ovarian cancer having routine CA-125 screening or other screening for this cancer [9]. Reasons for this include evidence that ambiguous test results are more likely to lead to further invasive, harmful, and unnecessary health care than they are likely to detect ovarian cancer in women who are at average risk of developing it.

Around 90% of women with advanced ovarian cancer have elevated levels of CA-125 in their blood serum, making CA-125 a useful tool for detecting ovarian cancer after the onset of symptoms [10]. Monitoring CA-125 blood serum levels is also useful for determining how ovarian cancer is responding to treatment (with the duration of disease-free survival correlating with the rate of fall of CA-125) [11] and for predicting a patient's prognosis after treatment [12]. This is because the persistence of high levels of CA-125 during therapy is associated with poor survival rates in patients. Also, an increase in CA-125 levels within individuals in a remission is a strong predictor of the recurrence of ovarian cancer [13]. Indeed, a rising CA-125 level may precede clinical evidence of disease relapse by an interval of 3 to 6 months.

Prognosis relates to both the initial and post-treatment CA-125 values. A preoperative value >65 U/mL suggests a poor prognosis. Persistent elevations following chemotherapy indicate a poor prognosis. The half-life of CA-125 after chemotherapy correlates with prognosis (patients with CA-125 half-life < 20 days show improved survival). Time-to-normalization (rate of fall of CA-125) affects prognosis with more rapid normalization within 3 cycles of chemotherapy correlating with improved survival [14].
In one case, elevated serum levels of CA-125 were observed in a male patient with IgE myeloma, however more cases are needed to determine the clinical significance of CA-125 in myeloma [15,16].

The potential role of CA-125 for the early detection of ovarian cancer is controversial and has not yet been adopted for widespread screening efforts in asymptomatic women [17,18]. The major issues with using the CA-125 biomarker are its lack of sensitivity, particularly for detecting early stages of ovarian cancer, and its lack of specificity, especially in premenopausal women [19]. These limitations mean that CA-125 testing often gives false positives for ovarian cancer and puts patients through unnecessary further screening (sometimes including surgery) and anxiety. Also, these limitations mean that many women with early stage ovarian cancer will receive a false negative from CA-125 testing and not get further treatment for their condition [20]. Therefore, we need to use some of novel methods for the sensitive detection of CA 125 [21–24].

In recent years, given the advancement of technology, the way for promotion of the rapid and sensitive diagnosis methods have been paved, and the dreams of disease prevention before treatment comes true. Regarding the public health issues, highly sensitive sensor systems are progressing for early stage detection of disease to reduce the risk of mortality [25,26]. Hence, the strong demand for employing cheap, efficient, fast, simple, portable and cost effective methods for immunoassay have been boosted. Among various detection tools, electrochemical methods, not only cover all these highlighted features, but also provide highly sensitive and selective biosensing strategies [27]. In order to achieve the lower detection limit and higher sensitivity, signal amplification techniques have been employed for amplifying response signal to detect low level of biomarkers for developing ultrasensitive electrochemical immunoassay [28].

Recently, signal amplification has been successfully performed by advanced nanomaterials. Nanoscale materials offer some outstanding prospects for interfacing biological recognition events with transduction of electronic signals and for designing new bioelectronic devices exhibiting with novel functions [29]. Since the discovery of the conducting polymers in 1977s by Shirakawa et al. [30], many scientists have been working on finding the applications for the newly introduced polymers and creating a pathway in the sensing world [31]. Thanks to their unique characters, including biocompatibility, fascinating electrocatalytic activity, large surface area, excellent conductivity, and stability, which is coupled with their ease of processing/modification and rich chemistry; polymer based electrochemical sensors were noticed as a promising platform for the sensitive detection of biological compounds [32–35]. The goal of the covalent attachment of biomolecules to the surface of polymer could be achieved by using polymer derivatives (e.g. carboxylated or aminated), which provides plentiful of functional groups for long term retention and stabilization of biomolecules [36–39]. Considering the electrochemical methods, electrochemical polymerization not only provide a simple and reproducible formation of polymer, but also permits easy control over the properties of the polymeric coating such as morphology and thickness, compare to other robust methods (e.g. chemicals) [40].

Graphene, a single atom thick layer of the ordered carbon in a honeycomb lattice structure, has stimulated research interests for the application in designing of versatile devices in the field of field emission, gas and biosensors, field effect transistors (FET), transparent electrodes and energy storage devices [41]. Because of the outstanding specifications such as simple synthesis, good water dispersibility, large accessible surface area, and good biocompatibility, graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (RGO) have found tremendous utility in the field of biosensing [42,43]. A variety of approaches have been reported based on employing EGO (alone or in combination with other materials as composites) in order to apply its superior performances in modifying surfaces, which results in enlargement of the surface area and acceleration of the electron transfer processes [44–48]. Several methods have been proposed for the reduction of graphene oxide to produce graphene, reduced graphene oxide or partially reduced graphene oxide. Chemical, thermal, electrochemical, photo thermal, photocatalytic, sonochemical and microwave are among the reported methods, each with its own advantages [49]. Seeking for the fast, simple, nontoxic and time efficient features leads us to applying the electrochemical reduction methods, which allows generating in situ reduced graphene oxide on the electrode surface [50,51].

The strategy of functionalizing bioactive materials on the surface of nanomaterial substrate to form advanced coating not only achieves synergistic effects but also improves the biocompatibility and bioactivity of the biomaterials. In this sense, poly-dopamine (PDA), which contains the catechol and amine functional groups, has attracted much interest. The structure of dopamine is a mimic of the specialized adhesive foot protein, Mytilus edulis foot protein-5 (Mefp-5). Numerous studies have shown that the coating of PDA can be used to modify the surface of rGO to show specific chemical properties, such as the suppression of nonspecific biological interactions or the promotion of specific ones. Therefore, we have developed a simple and environment-friendly approach for the preparation of rGO-PDA hybrid materials.

Taking the advantages of the above mentioned structures, an efficient deployment of a simple, inexpensive and sensor for the ultrasensitive electrochemical detection of CA 125 antigen has been reported. Poly dopamine (P(DA)), RGO, and cysteamine capped gold nanoparticle (Cys-AuNPs) were used for fabrication of an impressive modified layer for immobilization of HRP-labeled antibody to the electrode surface via an amide bond. Enhancing the conductivity of the substrate incorporated with increasing the surface area, improves the sensitivity and lowers the detection limit of the capture layer. Cyclic voltammetry (CV), and square wave voltammetry (SWV) were applied for the electrochemical recognition of the biolayer. The represented signals demonstrates excellent figure of merits and good capability of the engineered immunosensor towards sensitive detection of CA 125. Quantitative measurements of CA 125 in human plasma samples have been demonstrated, showing the potential of the practical application of this novel immunosensor for the analysis of CA 125 biomarker in human plasma samples.

N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-carbodiimide (EDC) and lithium perchlorate, disodium hydrogen phosphate, sodium chloride, potassium chloride and potassium di-hydrogen phosphate (the component of phosphate buffer saline solution buffer), sodium hydroxide (Merck, Germany), were from analytical reagent grade and used without further purification. Dopamine was obtained from Acros Organics, Germany. All solutions were prepared with deionized water (18.2 MΩ·cm, Zolalan Co., Iran). Human plasma samples were obtained from the Iranian Blood Transfusion Research Center (Tabriz, Iran). Human CA 125 ELISA kit (CanAg/CA125 EIA) including CA 125 antigen, antibody (anti CA 125) standard, standard buffer, and dilute solutions were obtained from CanAg Diagnostic AB Technology Co. (Gothenburg, Sweden).

Electrochemical measurements were recorded using a computer-controlled electrochemical system comprising of AUTOLAB system with PGSTAT302N (Eco Chemie, Utrecht, The Netherlands) using NOVA 1.11 software. The conventional three-electrode electrochemical cell was set using glassy carbon electrode (GCE), Ag/AgCl and a platinum wire as the working, reference, and counter electrodes, respectively. The surface morphology of the modified electrodes and the nanoparticles was evaluated with a FESEM (field emission scanning electron microscopy) analysis which was conducted on a TESCAN system of FEG-SEM MIRA3 TESCAN (Brno, Czech Republic). The elemental analysis of the electrode surface was recorded by energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) of MIRA3 TESCAN (Brno, Czech Republic) model.

In SWV investigations, the scan rate was adjusted for 0.05 V/s, and in SWV, the modulation amplitude was 50 mV, the step potential was 6 mV, and the apparent scan rate was 15 mV/s, in the potential range of −0.2 to 0.5 V. All measurements were the average of at least three replicates. Riboflavin (6,7-dimethyl-9-(d-1-ribityl)-isoalloxazine), is a water-soluble vitamin belonging to the B-complex group. Riboflavin (RF), is an essential precursor of two coenzymes: flavin mononucleotide and flavin adenosine dinucleotide. The electrochemical behavior of these coenzymes has been studied [54]. These compounds by accepting and donating two electrons in the isoalloxazine ring, show reversible two-electron reduction and oxidation waves both of which are due to over-lapping one-electron waves [54]. Electron transfer reaction upon reduction is as follows:

Prior to modification, the glassy carbon electrode (GCE; Azar electrode, Urmia; diameter 2 mm) was polished to a mirror luster on a 0.1 μm alumina slurry on a microcloth polishing pad, followed by sonication in an ethanol/water bath for 5 min and electrochemically cleaned by cycling in PBS 1× for 10 scans in the potential range of −0.2 to 1.0 V (scan rate 0.1 V/s), until reproducible voltammograms were obtained.

CV was used to form the thin film of PDA on the surface of GCE. For the preparation thin film of PDA on the surface of GCE, homogenous dopamine (0.005 g) was added to 5 mL PBS (pH = 7.4) and transferred to electrochemical cell. Then PDA films were electrodeposited onto GCE by cyclic voltammetry (CV) in the potential range from −0.6 to 0.5 V at a scan rate of 10 mV/s for 5 cycles (Fig. 1A). The first positive scan, an oxidation peak at +0.28 V was observed, which is attributed to the oxidation of the DA. In the subsequent reversal scan, one reduction peak was observed at 0.124 V, which may be attributed to the reduction of dopaminequinone (DAQ) and dopaminechrome (DAC) as well as the intramolecular cyclization reaction product of dopaminequinone (DAQ). Simultaneously, the anodic and cathodic peak current increased and decreased cycle by cycle, respectively. This reflecting the continuous growth of the film. When the cyclic potential scan reached 5 cycles, the peak currents hardly changed. These facts indicate that DA was successfully polymerized on the surface of GCE by the electropolymerization method. The polymerization mechanism of DA is described as follows: dopaminechrome (DAC) molecules partly may evolve to the more stable 5,6-dihydroindole molecules of two aromatic rings via isomerization, and 5,6-indolequinone is then electropolymerized to form polymers [52]. Scheme 1 shows electropolymerization of P(DA) at the surface of GCE.

It has been confirmed that monomer containing NH2 can be electropolymerized at the surface of electrode to form a conducting film via covalent bond (CN) between electrode and NH2. DA has two NH2 and two NH groups and that can easily be oxidized to produce NH2 covalent bond (CN) is then formed by the free radical reaction of NH2 with GCE surface with excellent stability. Therefore, the covalent bond (CN) might be formed between carbon electrode and the α-amino group of dopamine. It is important to point out that, during the low potential scan rate, the α-amino free radical will easily form and the polymerization of the P(DA) film could be formed on the surface of GCE (Scheme 2 ).

At the second step for preparation of modified electrode, the electrode was transfer to the electrochemical cell and electrodeposition of ERGO was performed. In this step, GO was synthesized from natural graphite powder by a modified Hummers method as previously reported in the literature [53]. The GCE pre-modified by P(DA) was subjected to 20 cycles of cyclic voltammetry at a scan rate of 10 mV/s in a potential range from −1.4 to 0 V in a 1 mg/mL GO solution containing 0.1 M lithium perchlorate (Fig. 1B). The ERGO/P(DA)/GCE were further modified with thiol (SH) groups by binding the COOH groups of ERGO to the amine (NH2) groups of CysA through amide bonds. The SH modified ERGO/P(DA)/GCE were used thereafter for electrochemical assembly of Au NPs. The prepared electrode (Cys A/ERGO/P(DA)/GCE) was undergoes the electrodeposition of gold nanoparticle in a solution containing 0.01 M HAuCL4 − and KCl in the potential of 0 V at 300 s (Fig. 1C). The modified electrode was denoted as Au NPs-Cys A/ERGO/P(DA)/GCE. For the preparation of immunosensor, 10 μL of 1 U/mL of HRP labeled anti CA 125 was casted on the surface of Au NPs-Cys A/ERGO/P(DA)/GCE and incubated in 4 °C for 5 h. Based on electrostatic interactions between the antibodies (HRP labeled anti CA 125) and the Au NPs, the anti-CA 125 was immobilized on the surface of electrode. Following anti-CA 125 incubation, the electrodes were thoroughly rinsed with wash buffer (0.1% Tween-PBS (pH 8.5)) to remove the unbound anti-CA 125 protein. Then BSA solution (3%) was added to the modified surface and left for 1 h to block the free sites of the electrode surfaces to minimize the non-specific binding. The immunosensor were then directly subjected to immunochemical reaction with CA 125 or to electrochemical measurements. For CA 125 sensing, 10 μL of CA 125 antigen were dropped onto the sensor surface and left for 1 h. Thereafter, it was washed with PBS to remove any unbound antibody molecules. The fabricated Ab (anti-CA 125)/Au NPs/Cys A/ERGO/P(DA)/GCE immunoelectrode was washed with PBS and stored at 4 °C until further use. Prior to electrochemical measurements, the immunosensor were washed thoroughly with wash buffer.

The surface morphology of the electrodeposited polymer on the electrode surface in different modification steps was investigated by FE-SEM (Fig. 2 ). As can be seen, polymeric chain was made on the surface of GCE after electropolymerization of P(DA) (Fig. 2A). Also, after modification of P(DA)-GCE by ERGO, a drastic increase in the surface roughness, compared to bare P(DA) (Fig. 2B), is good evidence that a uniform thickness of ERGO covered the whole scanning area of the electrode surface. In comparison to P(DA) film, the electrochemically reduced graphene oxide shows a rougher surface, which leads to the increasing of surface active sites and eventually affects the response sensitivities of the voltammetric measurements (Fig. 2C). The FE-SEM images of Au NPs-CysA/ERGO/P(DA)/GCE shows flower like gold nanoparticles with uniform structure, revealing that the Au NPs were indeed assembled onto the surface of CysA/ERGO/P(DA). Interestingly, more dispersed and less aggregated Au NSs are seen upon the electrostatic interactions of CysA/ERGO/P(DA) with the Au NPs. Possibly the binding of positively charged CysA/ERGO/P(DA) to the negatively charged Au NSs reduced the aggregation of the NPs through steric hindrance, although the loss of some Au NPs from the CysA/ERGO/P(DA) surface cannot be eliminated.

The coated layer provides a high surface area, which leads to enhance the antibody loading (Fig. 2D). A different morphology was developed over the electrode surface in the presence of antibody. The increased surface roughness implies antibody probe binding to the polymer.

The electrode modification steps were evaluated by SWV technique in 0.1 M RF containing NaCl which has been shown in Fig. 3 . The characteristic peak of bare GCE (curve a) was markedly increased after its modification with P(DA). Interestingly, the data obtained results clearly show that the combination of P(DA) and REGO definitely alter the nature of SWV oxidation peak current (low oxidation potential and high peak current). That might be related to the excellent property of P(DA)-ERGO, such as high specific surface area and electrical conductivity. As can be obtained from SWV curves, the oxidation peak current of P(DA)-GCE increased in accordance with the increase of the effective surface area after modifying REGO on the electrode proportional to bare GCE. Likewise, after surface thiolation of the P(DA)-ERGO, the oxidation peak current significantly increased, which shows that the desirable changes have been successfully applied on the surface of P(DA)-ERGO. Presence of a highly conductive ERGO layer on the P(DA)-GCE surface with a large surface area shows the signal amplification for the redox probe, due to the improvement of the electron transfer process. As it is obvious, changes in the current responses are completely distinguishable from SWVs, which highlights the SWV's capability in tracing small changes on this conductive platform in the presence of other reagents compare to the represented voltammograms in the same condition. To make a more stable and promising immunosensing platform further modification is required to create more binding sites to enhance sensitivity. Metal NPs provides a high surface area and abundant functional groups which improve their dispersivity, stability and sensitivity [55,56]. Thus, Cys A functionalized gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) were found more suitable for immunosensor application. Cys A and Au NPs covalently interact with each other via forming a covalent bond between the sulphur group of Cys and AuNPs [57,58]. Subsequently, Cys-AuNPs (capped) provide free amine group for interaction with a carboxylic group of Fc region of antibody via a covalent bond [59–61]. Thus, it provides a favorable condition for immobilization of the antibody against the desired analyte like cancer biomarker. Besides this, functionalized Au NPs enhanced the electrochemical response. The electrodeposition of the Au NPs on the surface of Cys A/ERGO/P(DA)/GCE led to a decrease in the peak current because of hindered electron transfer effect. Upon immobilization of HRP labeled-anti-CA 125, a decline in peak current was observed again (curve d). In fact, the antibodies act as an insulter and reduce the electron transfer rate. After blocking the nonspecific binding sites by BSA, the peak current further decreased. This could be attributed to the blocking effect of BSA on the electron transfer process. Following immersing the prepared electrode in solution of CA 125 antigen, the peak current was decreased again, because of the creation of the immunocomplex reaction.

The electroactive surface area (A) values, that are different from the geometric one of different electrodes, are measured by peak current versus scan rate (Fig. 4A–D). The values of different electrodes are calculated by CVs using the equation Γ =
Q nFA
. In the above equation, Γ is the surface coverage of the electrode reaction substance, Q is the charge involved in the reaction, which is calculated from the reduction peak area of the voltammogram by integrating the reduction current against time (Q = ∫idt). n is the number of electron transferred reaction, F is Faraday constant, and A is the electrode surface area. The A value of P(DA)-GCE and P(DA)-ERGO-GCE is 4.47 × 10−6 cm2 s−1 and 4.47 × 10−6 cm2 s−1, respectively, while P(DA)-ERGO-GCE modified GCE has a different active surface area with a value of 1.26 × 10−6 cm2 s−1, indicating that ERGO has a binding to P(DA) towards [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. AuPs increases the active surface area (6.12 × 10−6 cm2 s−1), and thus improves the electron transfer. Therefore, This composite (Au NPs-CysA-ERGO/P(DA)/GCE) alter the surface volume ratio and enhances the electro-catalytic activity. Finally, the diffusion layer thickness of Au NPs-CysA-ERGO/P(DA)/GCE leads to higher peak currents due to an increase in the particle size and active surface area with an A value of 7.35 × 10−6 cm2 s−1. Besides, as the microscopic area increases due to the presence of AuNPs, the available catalytic active sites increase.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical characteristic of the CA 125 immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimal experimental conditions, we studied the performance of this immunosensor to detect different concentrations of CA 125 and its SWV responses were recorded in the redox probe solution containing 0.01 M (RF and NaCl). As seen in Fig. 5 , well-defined SWV responses were observed in the presence of different concentrations of CA 125 and the peak current signals related to RF decrease with increase of the CA 125 concentration. This suggests that by adding different concentrations of CA 125 to be used as the target on the modified electrode surface, the antibody-antigen interaction was successfully performed. Due to immunocomplex between Ab-Ag the response of the redox probe (RF) is reduced and thereby leads to enhanced electron-transfer resistance. The following regression equation between the electrochemical current response and the CA 125 concentration range from 0.1 to 400 U/mL was found. The low limit of quantification for this immunosensor was 0.1 U/mL. Table 1 compares the analytical results of this work with the previous studies.

According to the obtained results, it was possible to apply this technique to the quantitative analysis of CA 125 in real samples using SWV technique (Fig. 6 ). As mentioned previously, the 0.01 RF and NaCl was selected as the supporting electrolyte for the quantification of CA 125. For this purpose, 0.5 mL of unprocessed human plasma samples were taken and added into 0.01 M (RF and NaCl) to reach a total volume of 5 mL.

Using SWV technique, the resulting sample solutions were measured using the prepared immunosensor. Excellent recoveries varying from 99.2% to 104.1% were obtained, which illustrates that the established method are quite desirable and qualified for practical sample testing.

To check the inter-electrode reproducibility of the modified electrode, four modified electrodes were tested simultaneously by recording SWVs in buffer solution, containing 100 U/mL of CA 125. The relative standard deviation was 4.33%. Therefore, CA 125/BSA/Ab/Au NPs/Cys A/ERGO-P(DA)-GCE imparts a suitable stability onto measurements of CA 125 by SWV techniques.

The stability of the developed method was investigated as a key factor affecting the analytical performance of the present immunosensor. In order to confirm the stability of the prepared immunosensor, we utilized the SWV technique to investigate voltammetric behavior. The voltammetric behavior of the proposed CA 125 immunosensor in the solution containing 0.01 M RF containing NaCl is very stable and no reduction in peak current related to RF is observed after 100 repetitive cycles (Fig. 7A & B). Additionally, the long-term stability of the immunosensor was studied during a 7-days period. After refrigeration (4 °C) for one week, the immunosensor was used to detect a CA 125 concentration of 1 μM. Refrigeration caused no significant change in stability and the immunosensor was seen to have preserved 96% of its initial activity (Fig. 7C & D). It can be proposed that the long term stability is related to the high chemical and mechanical stability of the P(DA)-ERGO-Cys A-Au NPs nanocomposites and covalent attachment of antibody functionalized AuNPs on the surface of the modified electrode; therefore, this nanocomposite indicates promising properties as a useful platform for immobilization of antibody and preparation of immunosensor.

Control experiments were performed in the presence of some coexistent proteins as interferences to evaluate the immunosensor selectivity. For this purpose, SWV experiments were carried out to check the cross-reactivity of the CA 125(100 U/mL) with possible coexisting and interfering compounds, such as BSA, p53, PSA, and CA 15-3. The prepared immunosensor (CA 125/BSA/Ab/Au NPs/Cys A/ERGO-P(DA)-GCE) was incubated with interfering compounds in the supporting electrolyte. The peak current produced by the CA 125/BSA/Ab/Au NPs/Cys A/ERGO-P(DA)-GCE is almost similar (Fig. 8 ). The successive addition of higher concentrations of interfering compounds did not make any appreciable increment in peak current, shows that the proposed sensor exhibits remarkable selectivity towards CA 125. This can be attributed to the suitable specificity of the CA 125/BSA/Ab/Au NPs/Cys A/ERGO-P(DA)-GCE immunosensor to detection of CA 125.

The recovery assessment of the CA-125 immunosensor was carried out by spiking serum samples with diverse concentrations of CA-125 (Table 2 ). The recovery of the electrode was calculated as percentage using the following equation:
R % =
spiked sample result – unspiked sample result
× 100 %
known spike added conccentration
Further, recovery ranges of the immunosensors were between 98.22 and 104.2% with RSD 1.18–4.56%, which are within acceptable range.

In summary, organic-based platform consist of poly dopamine and electrochemically reduced graphene-oxide nano-sheets for highly loading of CA 125 antibody was reported. ERGO combined with P(DA) enhances the performance of immunosensors in terms of sensitivity, wide dynamic range, low detection limit, good stability and impressive reusability incorporated with the capability to detection of target antigen. These advantages could make this platform as a new paradigm in immunosensor design. In general, a novel and selective electrochemical immunosensor utilizing a suitable nanocomposite (Au NPs/Cys A/ERGO/P(DA)) as a potentially pragmatic transduction platform on the surface of a modified electrode and riboflavin as an effective redox probe was developed. Riboflavin has been used in this work for the first time as an electrochemical indicator in the preparation of the electrochemical immunosensor for the detection of target protein (CA 125). The immunosensor was constructed by covalent immobilizing of a HRP-labeled antibody on the prepared interface. The presence of this nanocomposite with unique properties including prominent biocompatibility, good electrical conductivity, and vast surface area functions as a signal amplifier to facilitate the immobilization of the Ab on surface of the electrode. The proposed method has high sensitivity and stability with an estimated LOD of 150 pM and was used for diagnostics of antigen CA 125 in serum samples. Above all, the prepared aptasensor can be an extremely promising candidate applicable to and specific and convenient for, a wide range of electrochemical immunosensing applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support by Tabriz University of Medical Sciences.
